text: The surgeon begins the operation by incising the peritoneum from the lateral side towards the midline, revealing the hernia as a hole in the abdominal wall. The surrounding tissues are dissected off the peritoneal lining, which partially enters the hernia to form the hernia sac.
The surgeon performs a nearly bloodless dissection, revealing the peritoneal lining as it forms the hernia sac and extends into the hernia defect.

The surgeon retracts the hernia contents, including visible fat, back into the abdominal cavity. The pubis and Cooper's ligament are identified as anatomical landmarks for the medial point of dissection.

The surgeon has reduced the hernia sac and its contents back into the abdominal cavity. A piece of self-fixating mesh is placed over the hernia defect, adhering to the abdominal wall without the need for stitches or tacks. The mesh is carefully positioned to ensure it lays flat against the abdominal wall.

The surgeon closes the peritoneum over the mesh, adding a protective layer to prevent adhesion to the intestines. The hernia sac and fat are positioned against this closure to help prevent hernia recurrence.
The surgeon uses a locking absorbable suture to secure the closure, running the suture back on itself before cutting it to complete the operation.
